questions.to_find: Number of distinct values that B can possibly take after P1 and P2 execute concurrently

understand.plain: Two functions P1 and P2 share a variable B initialized to 2. Since there is no synchronization, their individual statements can interleave in any order. We must enumerate all possible interleavings and find how many distinct final values B can have.

keywords.explain: When two concurrent processes access a shared variable without synchronization, the final result depends on the order of execution (interleaving).
keywords.example: If P1 reads B before P2 writes it, P1 uses the old value; but if P2 writes first, P1 uses the new value.

keywords.explain: The possible orderings in which atomic statements from two concurrent processes can execute.
keywords.example: With 2 statements each, there are C(4,2) = 6 possible interleavings of their statements.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='230' height='110' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='125' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P1()</text><text x='125' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s1: C = B - 1</text><text x='125' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s2: B = 2 * C</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='230' height='110' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P2()</text><text x='395' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s3: D = 2 * B</text><text x='395' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s4: B = D - 1</text><rect x='10' y='140' width='500' height='105' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Possible Final Values of B</text><text x='260' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sequential P1 then P2: B = 3</text><text x='260' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sequential P2 then P1: B = 4</text><text x='260' y='228' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Interleaved (s1,s3,s2,s4) etc: B = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: P1 has two statements: C=B-1 and B=2*C. P2 has two statements: D=2*B and B=D-1. B starts at 2. All 6 interleavings are enumerated to find distinct values.

given.aim: Find how many distinct values B can have after P1 and P2 complete execution concurrently.

terms.connects: Both P1 and P2 read and write B; final value depends on interleaving order
P1 reads B in s1 and writes B in s2
P2 reads B in s3 and writes B in s4

given.plan: Enumerate all C(4,2)=6 possible interleavings of the 4 statements (s1,s2 from P1 and s3,s4 from P2, preserving each process's internal order). Compute B's final value in each case and count distinct results.

steps.title: List all valid interleavings (s1 before s2, s3 before s4)
steps.apply:
The 6 orderings are:
1. s1,s2,s3,s4 (P1 first, then P2)
2. s3,s4,s1,s2 (P2 first, then P1)
3. s1,s3,s2,s4
4. s1,s3,s4,s2
5. s3,s1,s2,s4
6. s3,s1,s4,s2
steps.note: Within each process, statement order must be preserved (s1 before s2 for P1; s3 before s4 for P2)

steps.title: Compute B for each interleaving
steps.note: In interleavings 4 and 6, P2's write to B (s4) happens before P1's write (s2), so P1 overwrites P2's result

steps.title: Count distinct values
steps.apply: Results: {3, 4, 3, 2, 3, 2} → distinct values are {2, 3, 4} → 3 distinct values
steps.note: B=2 appears in interleavings 4 and 6; B=3 appears in 1,3,5; B=4 appears in interleaving 2 only

questions.formula_note: Race condition analysis via exhaustive interleaving enumeration. With n atomic statements from two processes (m from P1, k from P2), there are C(m+k, m) valid interleavings, each potentially yielding a different final shared-variable value.

questions.answer: B. The proposed solution guarantees mutual exclusion but fails to prevent deadlock.
questions.to_find: Which property (mutual exclusion, freedom from deadlock) the given synchronization construct satisfies

understand.plain: Process X sets varP=true and waits while varQ is true before entering critical section; Process Y sets varQ=true and waits while varP is true. This is a flag-based synchronization attempt. We need to check if mutual exclusion is guaranteed (at most one in CS at a time) and if deadlock is possible (both stuck waiting forever).

keywords.explain: At most one process is inside the critical section at any given time.
keywords.example: If X is in CS, Y must not be in CS simultaneously.

keywords.explain: A situation where two or more processes are each waiting for the other to release a resource, resulting in all of them being stuck forever.
keywords.example: X sets varP=true and waits for varQ=false; Y sets varQ=true and waits for varP=false. Both wait forever.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='230' height='90' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='125' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Process X</text><text x='125' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>varP = true</text><text x='125' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>while (varQ == true) wait</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='230' height='90' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Process Y</text><text x='395' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>varQ = true</text><text x='395' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>while (varP == true) wait</text><rect x='10' y='120' width='500' height='85' rx='8' class='no'/><text x='260' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>DEADLOCK SCENARIO</text><text x='260' y='168' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>X sets varP=true, Y sets varQ=true (concurrent)</text><text x='260' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>X waits for varQ=false, Y waits for varP=false → STUCK</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Process X sets varP=true and loops while varQ is true. Process Y sets varQ=true and loops while varP is true. If both set their flags before checking the other's flag, both spin forever — deadlock.

given.aim: Determine whether the synchronization construct guarantees mutual exclusion and/or freedom from deadlock.

terms.meaning: Boolean flag for Process X, initialized to false
terms.example: varP = true signals X wants to enter CS
terms.connects: Process Y checks varP in its while-loop before entering CS

terms.meaning: Boolean flag for Process Y, initialized to false
terms.example: varQ = true signals Y wants to enter CS
terms.connects: Process X checks varQ in its while-loop before entering CS

terms.meaning: X enters CS only when varQ == false; Y enters CS only when varP == false
terms.example: X can only be in CS if varQ is false, meaning Y is not trying
terms.connects: This mutual exclusion check ensures they cannot both be in CS simultaneously

given.plan: Check mutual exclusion: Can X and Y both be in CS simultaneously? Check deadlock: Can X and Y both be stuck in their while-loops forever?

steps.title: Check Mutual Exclusion
steps.formula_raw: X in CS => varQ == false; Y in CS => varP == false
steps.apply: For X to be in CS: the while(varQ==true) loop must have exited, meaning varQ==false at that moment, meaning Y has varQ=false. If Y is also in CS simultaneously, Y must have passed its while(varP==true) loop, meaning varP==false. But X set varP=true before checking varQ. So varP=true and varQ=false for X in CS, and varP=false and varQ=true for Y in CS — these are contradictory. Both cannot be in CS at the same time. Mutual exclusion IS guaranteed.
steps.note: The flags act as mutual exclusion guards — if X is in CS (varQ was false), Y cannot enter because varP is still true (set by X before entering)

steps.title: Check for Deadlock
steps.apply: Interleaving: X executes varP=true (varP=true, varQ=false), then Y executes varQ=true (varP=true, varQ=true). Now X checks while(varQ==true) — true, so X waits. Y checks while(varP==true) — true, so Y waits. Both are stuck in their while-loops forever. Neither can set their flag to false (that only happens inside the CS body). This is a deadlock.
steps.note: This is a classic example of a two-flag protocol without a tie-breaking mechanism (unlike Peterson's algorithm which uses a 'turn' variable to break ties)

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Mutual exclusion: YES (guaranteed). Deadlock freedom: NO (deadlock is possible). This matches option B.
steps.note: Peterson's algorithm fixes this by adding a 'turn' variable so that when both processes want to enter simultaneously, one of them gets priority and proceeds

solution.result: B. The proposed solution guarantees mutual exclusion but fails to prevent deadlock.

questions.formula_note: Two-flag synchronization protocol analysis. Mutual exclusion holds because entry requires the other's flag to be false; deadlock occurs because both can set their flags before checking, creating a circular wait with no tie-breaking mechanism (contrast with Peterson's algorithm).
